title: Mediterranean
subtitle: Greek and Roman antiquity — the classical forms that Europe keeps returning to.
intro: The classical world's obsession with the human body, proportion, and idealized form shaped every European revival that came after — Renaissance, Neoclassical, and on. Much of it survives only in fragments.

works.title: Palace of Knossos
works.desc: A sprawling Bronze Age palace with running water, frescoes of dolphins and bull-leapers, and a storage maze that may be the origin of the Minotaur myth.

works.title: The Parthenon
works.desc: Temple to Athena on the Acropolis. The columns lean inward and swell slightly in the middle — optical corrections so they look perfectly straight.

works.title: Charioteer of Delphi
works.desc: One of the few surviving Greek bronzes. His calm expression, inset with glass and copper eyes, is a moment in a victory lap — all force held still.

works.title: Discobolus
works.desc: Myron's discus thrower — the original bronze is lost; only Roman marble copies survive. A moment just before release, coiled and still.

works.title: Venus de Milo
works.desc: Marble Aphrodite found on the island of Milos in 1820. The missing arms are part of her fame — we don't know what she was doing.

works.title: Winged Victory of Samothrace
works.desc: Nike landing on the prow of a ship, wind pressing her drapery against her body. One of the most physically dynamic pieces of ancient sculpture.

works.title: Laocoön and His Sons
works.desc: A Trojan priest and his sons strangled by sea serpents. Dug up in a vineyard in 1506 and hauled to the Vatican. Michelangelo studied it for weeks.

works.title: Augustus of Prima Porta
works.desc: The emperor posed as a general, barefoot like a god. The breastplate shows the return of captured Roman standards — a diplomatic win dressed as conquest.

works.title: The Colosseum
works.desc: An elliptical amphitheater for 50,000 spectators. The underground hypogeum ran elevators, flooded the floor for sea battles, and housed wild animals.

works.title: Trajan's Column
works.desc: A 100-foot marble column whose spiral frieze narrates the Dacian Wars in 155 continuous scenes — history carved as a comic strip.

works.title: Pompeii
works.desc: Vesuvius sealed a whole Roman town in volcanic ash. Frescoes, bread loaves, graffiti, and the shapes of bodies all survived — daily life frozen.

works.title: Riace Bronzes
works.desc: Two life-size bronze warriors pulled from the sea off Calabria in 1972. Their teeth are silver, their eyelashes hammered copper.

works.title: Aphrodite of Knidos
works.desc: The first free-standing nude female figure in Greek sculpture — scandalous enough that men climbed her sanctuary walls at night. Only copies survive.

works.title: Kroisos Kouros
works.desc: A young man stepping forward, hands at his sides, slight smile — the archaic formula. Found on a grave at Anavyssos, one leg at a time.

works.title: Ara Pacis
works.desc: The Altar of Peace. The imperial family processes in carved marble, framed by acanthus tendrils. Augustus inserts himself into the mythic past.

works.title: Pantheon
works.desc: The largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The oculus at its center has been open to the sky for nineteen centuries — rain falls through it into drains in the floor.

works.title: Apoxyomenos
works.desc: An athlete scraping oil from his arm. The Roman marble copy stands in for a lost bronze original by Alexander the Great's favorite sculptor.

works.title: Sarcophagus of the Spouses
works.desc: A painted terracotta couple reclining together on their tomb lid — smiling, touching, treated as equals. Unthinkable in contemporary Greek or Egyptian funerary art.

works.title: Doryphoros
works.desc: The "Spear Bearer" — a treatise on ideal human proportion made in marble. Polykleitos wrote a book called the Canon about it; only this statue survives.

works.title: Dying Gaul
works.desc: A defeated Celt collapsing on his shield, wound dripping. Pergamene art showed the enemy as human — a compliment paid in marble, after the kill.

works.title: Boxer at Rest
works.desc: A bronze boxer after a fight — cauliflower ears, bloodied lip, exhausted. One of the few surviving Greek originals, not a later copy.

works.title: Boy with Thorn
works.desc: A boy pulling a thorn from his foot, head bent in total concentration. One of the first ancient bronzes to be widely copied in the Renaissance.

works.title: Hadrian's Villa
works.desc: A 250-acre retreat where the emperor built scale models of the sights from his tour of the empire — a Nile canal, an Athenian stoa, a Canopus.

works.title: Kritios Boy
works.desc: The hinge between Archaic and Classical — the first marble body where weight shifts onto one leg (contrapposto), giving the figure life.

works.title: Artemision Bronze
works.desc: Pulled from a Roman shipwreck off Cape Artemision. Either Zeus throwing a thunderbolt or Poseidon throwing a trident — his missing weapon makes it impossible to say.

works.title: Erechtheion
works.desc: Temple on the sacred north side of the Acropolis. Six marble maidens (the Caryatids) hold up the porch in place of columns.

works.title: Alexander Mosaic
works.meta: Roman copy of Hellenistic painting · c. 100 BCE · Pompeii
works.desc: Alexander charging Darius at Issus, rendered in a million tiny tesserae. The lost original oil painting was probably by Philoxenos of Eretria.

works.title: Lady of Elche
works.desc: A limestone bust with enormous spoked headdress-wheels over the ears. Iberian, not Greek — the style belongs to a world that left no writing to explain itself.

works.title: Mask of Agamemnon
works.desc: A beaten-gold funerary mask from Grave Circle A at Mycenae. Schliemann announced he had "looked upon the face of Agamemnon" — though this mask is 300 years too early.

works.title: Capitoline Wolf
works.meta: Etruscan or Medieval bronze · 5th c. BCE or 13th c. CE
works.desc: The she-wolf nursing Romulus and Remus — emblem of Rome. Once thought ancient Etruscan; recent analysis suggests a medieval re-casting.

works.title: Chimera of Arezzo
works.desc: Bronze of the mythical lion-goat-snake beast, wounded and snarling. Found in 1553 and immediately cleaned up by Cellini; the inscription on its leg is Etruscan, not Latin.

works.title: Pergamon Altar
works.desc: A colossal marble frieze of the gods fighting the giants — deep-cut writhing bodies that may have inspired Michelangelo. The whole structure was hauled to Berlin in 1880.

works.title: Barberini Faun
works.desc: A sleeping satyr sprawled drunk on a rock, legs splayed, utterly without dignity. Excavated from Hadrian's mausoleum moat — perhaps thrown over the walls during a siege.

works.title: Portland Vase
works.desc: A cobalt-blue glass vase dipped in white and carved back through the outer layer. One of the finest surviving pieces of Roman cameo glass — and inspiration for Wedgwood's first portraits.

works.title: Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
works.desc: The only surviving large Roman bronze equestrian. It lived because Christians mistook it for Constantine. Now replaced by a copy in the Campidoglio; the original is inside.

works.title: Parthenon Frieze
works.meta: Classical Greek · Pheidias workshop · c. 440 BCE
works.desc: A continuous band 160 meters long, showing the Panathenaic procession — horses, musicians, elders, gods receiving the city's tribute. Half survives in the British Museum, half in Athens.

works.title: Herculaneum
works.desc: Pompeii's smaller, richer neighbor. Volcanic mud sealed it instead of ash, so wooden beams, baby cradles, and carbonized scrolls all survived.

works.title: Borghese Gladiator
works.desc: Not actually a gladiator — a warrior fighting a mounted opponent, arm raised with a (lost) shield. A tour de force of anatomical study; every muscle is taut with the effort of the moment before impact.

works.title: Sleeping Hermaphroditus
works.meta: Roman copy of a Greek original · 2nd century CE · Sully Wing
works.desc: From one angle, a sleeping woman. Walk around to the other side: the body is both. The mattress beneath — realistic enough that you want to touch it — was carved by Bernini in 1619 when the statue was restored.

works.title: House of the Faun
works.desc: One of the largest residences in Pompeii, three acres across. Its atrium held the bronze dancing faun that gave it the name; the floor held the Alexander Mosaic.

works.title: Nike of Paionios
works.meta: Classical Greek · Paionios · c. 420 BCE · Olympia
works.desc: The Messenians and Naupactians dedicated her at Olympia after a victory over Sparta. Nike lands on a plinth, wind pressing drapery against her body — 170 years before the Winged Victory of Samothrace.

works.title: New York Kouros
works.desc: One of the earliest freestanding Greek marble nudes, Attic. Still stiff with Egyptian influence — striding left foot, clenched fists — but the archaic smile has arrived.

works.title: Moschophoros (Calf Bearer)
works.desc: A man carries a calf to sacrifice; both smile — the archaic grin. His tunic is incised rather than carved, an early convention already on its way out.

works.title: Ludovisi Throne
works.desc: A marble three-sided relief of uncertain function (a throne? an altar?). On its main panel, Aphrodite rising from the sea, draped in a wet gown that might as well not be there.

works.title: Apollo Belvedere
works.desc: A marble copy of a lost Greek bronze, long considered the pinnacle of male beauty. Winckelmann helped launch Neoclassicism by writing about this single statue.

works.title: Antikythera Mechanism
works.desc: A corroded bronze device pulled from a Roman shipwreck in 1901. CT scans finally revealed: a geared orrery predicting sun, moon, and five planets. No surviving match until the Renaissance.

works.title: Dipylon Amphora
works.desc: A grave marker nearly five feet tall, painted with stylized mourners in a prothesis scene. The moment figurative imagery returns to Greek pottery after five centuries of pure geometry.

works.title: Treasury of Atreus
works.desc: A tholos tomb — a stone beehive thirteen meters high, held up only by its own corbelled weight. For fifteen centuries, the largest dome in the world.

works.title: Arch of Titus
works.desc: Triumphal arch for the sack of Jerusalem. Its inner relief shows the Menorah and other temple spoils being carried in procession — our earliest image of the Second Temple's contents.

works.title: Library of Celsus at Ephesus
works.desc: Two-story facade of a library that held 12,000 scrolls and the sarcophagus of its founder in a chamber behind the wall — a library as a tomb.

works.title: Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
works.desc: Tomb of the Carian satrap Mausolus, so grand that his name became the word for any lavish tomb. Wonder of the ancient world; shaken down by earthquakes; its fragments in the British Museum.

works.title: Phaistos Disc
works.desc: A fired clay disk stamped with 241 signs in a spiral. Still undeciphered. One of the earliest known uses of movable type — by more than 2,500 years.

works.title: Euphronios Krater
works.meta: Archaic Greek · Euphronios & Euxitheos · c. 515 BCE
works.desc: A red-figure calyx-krater showing Hypnos and Thanatos carrying Sarpedon's body. Looted from an Etruscan tomb; caught up in a 30-year dispute; finally returned to Italy in 2008.

works.title: Temple of Aphaia
works.meta: Late Archaic Greek · c. 500 BCE · Aegina
works.desc: A limestone temple whose pedimental sculptures, now in Munich, capture the hinge between Archaic stiffness and Classical naturalism — look at the east and west together to see a style changing.

works.title: Temple of Poseidon at Sounion
works.desc: White marble columns on a cape where the sea drops 60 meters away on three sides. Byron carved his name into one of the columns in 1810.

works.title: Temple of Zeus at Olympia
works.desc: Once held the chryselephantine Zeus — one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world, carved by Phidias. The temple fell in an earthquake; the Zeus was hauled to Constantinople and burned.

works.title: Hagia Triada Sarcophagus
works.desc: A painted limestone sarcophagus showing funeral rites on all four sides — a bull sacrifice, women carrying pails of blood, the deceased receiving offerings. Our clearest window onto Minoan ritual.

works.title: Roman Forum
works.meta: Roman Republic → Imperial · 7th c. BCE – 4th c. CE
works.desc: The civic heart of ancient Rome — law courts, speaker's platforms, temples, triumphal arches, all crammed into a low strip between the Palatine and Capitoline hills.

works.title: Obelisk of Theodosius
works.meta: Egyptian carved 15th c. BCE, re-erected 390 CE · Istanbul
works.desc: An Egyptian obelisk Thutmose III set up at Karnak; Theodosius hauled it to Constantinople and planted it on a marble base carved with Byzantine court scenes. Two monuments in one.

works.title: Bull-Leaping Fresco
works.desc: Three figures vault over a charging bull on the upper east wing of Knossos: the dark-skinned leaper mid-somersault, pale women stationed before and behind. Whether ritual, sport, or myth, it remains the iconic image of Minoan Crete.

works.title: Snake Goddess
works.desc: A bare-breasted figurine gripping writhing snakes, her flounced skirt falling in tight registers, a feline perched on her head. Found in the palace 'Temple Repository' pits at Knossos, she is probably a household deity or priestess rather than a goddess.

works.title: Akrotiri Frescoes
works.desc: A Bronze Age town buried by the Thera eruption in meters of ash — its plastered rooms preserved images of saffron-picking girls, fishermen, and a miniature harbor fleet. When excavation began in 1967 the colors were still bright.

works.title: Kamares Ware
works.desc: Thin-walled polychrome pottery painted with rippling tendrils and marine forms on a glossy black ground. Named after the sacred cave on Mount Ida where the first examples were found tossed as offerings.

works.title: Prince of the Lilies
works.desc: A reconstructed relief fresco of a figure crowned with peacock-feathered tiara striding through a field of lilies. Arthur Evans combined three scattered plaster fragments into his imagined 'Priest-King' — later scholars think the pieces likely belong to different figures entirely.

works.title: Apollo of Veii
works.desc: A full-size polychrome terracotta from the roofline of the Portonaccio sanctuary, striding forward to contest Herakles for a stolen deer. Its broad smile and swinging drapery put Etruscan sculpture at parity with anything being made in archaic Greece.

works.title: Tomb of the Leopards
works.meta: Etruscan painted tomb · c. 480 BCE · Tarquinia
works.desc: A small banquet chamber whose back wall shows two leopards flanking a lintel, below which men recline on couches while flute and lyre players serenade them. The brilliant red-and-black palette and easy joy have no parallel in surviving Greek painting.

works.title: Tomb of the Diver
works.desc: The only known example of Greek figural tomb painting from the classical period. A young man dives into water beneath a lone tree — possibly a metaphor for the leap into the afterlife; the side walls show a genial symposium underway.

works.title: François Vase
works.meta: Attic black-figure · c. 570 BCE · signed Kleitias/Ergotimos
works.desc: A volute krater dense with 270 named figures in six stacked registers — Achilles ambushing Troilus, the marriage of Peleus and Thetis, the Calydonian boar hunt. Found in fragments in an Etruscan tomb near Chiusi in 1844.

works.title: Maison Carrée
works.desc: The best-preserved Roman temple anywhere, built by Agrippa for the imperial cult. Thomas Jefferson obsessed over its proportions and based the Virginia State Capitol on it, launching the Roman revival in American civic architecture.

works.title: Pont du Gard
works.meta: Roman aqueduct · 1st century CE · southern France
works.desc: A three-tiered limestone aqueduct carrying water fifty kilometers to Nîmes across the Gardon valley. Each block weighs up to six tonnes and was set without mortar; the gradient over the full course drops only 12.6 meters.

works.title: Arch of Constantine
works.desc: A triumphal arch cobbled together from reused second-century reliefs of Trajan, Hadrian, and Marcus Aurelius — a frank admission that fourth-century sculptors could no longer match the earlier skill. The recycled figures were even recut with Constantine's head.

works.title: Baths of Caracalla
works.desc: A 25-hectare bathing complex that could hold 1,600 people at once, heated by underfloor hypocausts stoked by armies of slaves. The painted vaults fell centuries ago but Shelley wrote much of Prometheus Unbound wandering the ruins.

works.title: Pula Arena
works.meta: Roman amphitheater · 1st century CE · Istria, Croatia
works.desc: One of only six surviving Roman amphitheaters that retain a complete outer wall. Seating 23,000 for gladiatorial combat, the limestone ring still hosts opera and concerts under the open sky each summer.

works.title: Diocletian's Palace
works.desc: A retired emperor's fortified seaside retreat built on the plan of a military camp. Its peristyle, mausoleum (now the cathedral), and substructures form the kernel around which the medieval city of Split grew.

works.title: Leptis Magna
works.meta: Roman provincial city · expanded 193 CE · Libya
works.desc: Hometown of Septimius Severus and the showpiece of Roman Africa, with its four-way arch, Severan basilica, and enormous forum paved in imported marble. Desert sand buried and preserved it after the Arab conquest.

works.title: Villa of the Mysteries
works.meta: Roman frescoed villa · c. 60 BCE · Pompeii
works.desc: Life-size figures on a crimson ground enact what looks like the initiation of a bride into a Dionysian cult — a flagellation, a satyr's mask, a winged figure about to whip a kneeling woman. No one is sure whether the scene is myth, rite, or both.

works.title: Temple of Apollo at Delphi
works.desc: The seat of the Oracle. Inscribed on its wall: "Know thyself." Six of the original 38 Doric columns still stand at the edge of the sacred precinct.

works.title: Heraion of Samos
works.desc: Sanctuary of Hera near her birthplace. Four successive temples built on the same footprint; the fourth was the largest Greek temple ever begun — never finished. One column standing.

works.title: Propylaea of the Acropolis
works.desc: The ceremonial gateway to the Acropolis. An unfinished masterpiece — the Peloponnesian War stopped construction; beam sockets on the unfinished wings still wait for marble that never came.
